alcatraz-island-microgrid.summary: Alcatraz Island's National Park Service power system is an off-grid solar-battery-diesel microgrid commissioned in 2012 to replace diesel-only service. The as-built record identifies 305 kW of SunPower PV, a 1.9 MWh East Penn gel lead-acid battery, eight Princeton Power 100 kW bidirectional inverters, two 250 kW MTU diesel generators, and Princeton controls. DOE reports approximately 400,000 kWh of annual savings and a roughly 75% reduction in diesel delivered to the island after installation, while later NREL analysis found that generator-led battery charging was still curtailing PV and raising fuel and battery costs.
alcatraz-island-microgrid.status: The microgrid remains an active NPS/NLR optimization project, but its lifecycle has included material failures. A federal procurement account says the battery system failed and was replaced in late 2018, and an October 2019 energy-management failure left the two diesel generators supplying all power until repairs. NLR's page, updated 23 April 2026, says NLR was working with NPS and Princeton Power to implement and measure a revised load-following strategy. Public sources reviewed do not establish when that implementation finished or disclose current component availability, so uninterrupted full-hybrid operation should not be assumed.
alcatraz-island-microgrid.historicalLocation: Alcatraz Island, San Francisco, CA, United States

equipment.detail: Coordinates solar, battery, and diesel resources and starts diesel generation when renewable energy and stored energy cannot meet load.
The archived manufacturer case study identifies eight 100 kW-class power-conversion units; the federal page does not restate the model count.
Lead-acid battery bank. The reviewed sources do not disclose a battery manufacturer or cell model.
959 modules totaling approximately 305 kW on the Cellhouse roof.
Roof-mounting system used for the Cellhouse array.

Eight 100 kW units: four interfacing the PV array and four interfacing the battery. The modular arrangement allowed individual units to be taken offline while the system continued at reduced capacity.
480 gel VRLA lead-acid cells in two 240-cell strings, approximately 480 V and 1.9 MWh. The manufacturer/model of the late-2018 replacement was not disclosed in the sources reviewed.
Two 250 kW generator sets supplied by Valley Power Systems using MTU equipment.
Site controller/EMOS coordinated PV, battery, and diesel dispatch. The federal repair account identifies the EMOS and battery-management system as repair and retuning scope after the 2019 failure.

technicalDetails.detail: Lead Acid Battery Bank
The controller prioritizes photovoltaic production, then battery energy, and calls diesel generation when those resources cannot support island load.
DOE reports a verified 305 kW array, while the recovered directory listed 400 kW; both values are preserved with provenance instead of silently selecting one.
Public federal sources do not identify the PV-module, battery-cell, diesel-genset, switchgear, or communications-network model numbers.

NPS describes PV serving the load first, batteries covering the next increment, and diesel starting last; excess PV and excess diesel generation can charge the batteries. The island has no mainland utility connection.

The 2012 technical account reports an August 2012 battery-string ground fault and an inverter component failure attributed to condensation. Firmware was being tuned roughly every six weeks during early commissioning.

After a March 2016 site tour, NREL estimated an ideal annual O&M budget of $90,000-$100,000, including monitoring, three battery-test visits, and two PV-cleaning visits. It recorded planning assumptions of eight years for battery replacement, ten years for inverters, and seven years for the battery-management system; these were planning intervals, not verified replacement dates.

A federal sole-source repair justification, available through a procurement mirror, says the battery system failed and was replaced in late 2018. It also says an October 2019 failure required removing the system from EMOS control, leaving two generators to supply all island power while repairs were arranged.

REopt found that cycle-charging the battery from diesel left too little capacity to absorb midday PV. A load-following strategy was projected to cut PV curtailment from about 30% to 4%, halve fuel consumption and cost by about 15,000 gallons per year, and save approximately $115,000 annually in diesel and battery wear; these are modeled opportunities, not measured realized savings.

A 2023 DOI Inspector General evaluation found that NPS could not consistently support required biannual rooftop-array inspections or weekly PV production reports under its concession contracts. This is an oversight and documentation finding, not proof that those activities never occurred.

No reviewed source discloses the 2018 replacement battery manufacturer, chemistry, model, or present energy capacity; the completion date and measured benefit of the control change described by NLR in April 2026 are also not yet public.

amjaras-hybrid-project.summary: The Amdjarass (also spelled Amdjeres or Am Djerass) town power system in Chad has evolved from a 1.1 MW Vergnet wind-diesel microgrid into a wind-solar-battery-diesel system. Four 275 kW wind turbines were delivered in 2016. InnoVent says a separate 5.124 MWp, 8,400-module tracker PV plant with 10 MWh of batteries began injecting power on 25 September 2025 alongside four 1 MVA generator sets, for a town load then no greater than 1 MW. The reviewed sources do not publish a measured annual renewable-energy share.
amjaras-hybrid-project.status: Operating as an expanded hybrid town grid. The original four-turbine, 1.1 MW wind plant is still reported operational. InnoVent reported first PV injection on 25 September 2025, final testing and transformer work in progress, and official completion planned for late November 2025; Le Monde reported in February 2026 that Amdjarass had no routine power cuts and that the solar plant had been inaugurated in October 2025. Vergnet/SOTAFRIC announcements in 2021-2023 described four additional wind turbines, but later 2025-2026 accounts still identify only four turbines, so completion of that wind expansion is not established.

equipment.detail: Four 275 kW turbines, 1.1 MW total. The model designation is reported by the current The Wind Power project database and is consistent with the contemporary four-by-275 kW project record.
Controller monitors real-time grid demand and dispatches wind, solar, batteries, and generator sets, including source connection/disconnection and spinning-reserve management.
8,400 modules rated 610 W each on 120 solar trackers. InnoVent does not identify module or tracker manufacturers/models.
Five systems rated 2 MWh each, 10 MWh total. Manufacturer, model, power rating, chemistry, and usable capacity are not disclosed.
Four units rated 1 MVA each. InnoVent does not identify manufacturer, model, fuel, or power factor.

technicalDetails.detail: The wind-farm is located on a plateau near Amdjarass at a height of about 1000m a.s.l.

Vergnet says the Government of Chad received the 1.1 MW four-turbine phase in February 2016. A January 2017 contract above EUR 8 million then proposed grid-forming high-capacity storage and controls intended to enable 100% renewable operation.

Vergnet/SOTAFRIC described an extension with four new turbines, 2.5 MW of solar, and 2.5 MW of generators. A May 2023 market report said civil works for four additional turbines were complete and completion was expected that June. Later project accounts in 2025-2026 still describe only four turbines, so the additional-turbine phase should remain classified as announced rather than verified complete.

ADERM described a 5.4 MW solar plant and '10 megawatts' of batteries while construction was underway in March 2025. InnoVent's later commissioning account gives the more specific as-built ratings of 5.124 MWp and 10 MWh; the differing units and project stage are preserved rather than treated as equivalent.

The PV plant first injected power on 25 September 2025. InnoVent then expected a 15/33 kV transformer by the end of October, followed by final tests and formal completion in late November; the associated 33 kV network was intended to reach the airport and a planned slaughterhouse.
InnoVent planned to support the first year of operation, after which ADERM could operate independently or renew the support arrangement. No later public source found states which option was selected.

Le Monde reported in February 2026 that Amdjarass did not experience the routine power cuts seen elsewhere and attributed supply to four wind turbines and a solar plant inaugurated in October 2025. This is a reported service outcome, not a quantified availability or renewable-share measurement.

No source reviewed provides PV or battery vendor/model data, BESS power or chemistry, current generator make/model, measured generation by source, fuel savings, load growth, or independent acceptance-test results. The relationship between the 2017 storage contract, the 2021-2023 Vergnet/SOTAFRIC extension, and the 2025 InnoVent solar-BESS plant is not fully documented.

andaman-island-indian-coast-guard.summary: Optimal Power Solutions commissioned a remote solar-battery-diesel microgrid for the Indian Coast Guard in the Andaman Islands in 2014. The site-specific commissioning record gives 75 kWp of PV, a 240 V/2,400 Ah battery bank, two 30 kVA diesel generators, two proprietary 20 kVA parallel inverter modules, and a Station Control Module with remote monitoring. A later OPS portfolio page separately describes a broader eight-system Coast Guard island portfolio totaling 320 kWp; the relationship between that aggregate and the 75 kWp commissioning record is not explained.
andaman-island-indian-coast-guard.status: Commissioned in October 2014 according to the system supplier. OPS continues to present the Coast Guard island microgrids in its current project portfolio, and a 2024 Indian power-sector report lists the 75 kW project, but neither source establishes present operation, availability, or component condition. No site-specific failure, replacement, expansion, performance, or decommissioning update was located through July 2026, so current operating status is unknown.

Coordinates battery, load, and diesel-generator operation and provides remote monitoring.
AC switching cabinet, DC distribution board, and PV combiner boxes were included. Their manufacturers and models are not disclosed.
The public commissioning account does not identify the PV module, battery, or diesel-generator manufacturers/models or the battery chemistry.

technicalDetails.detail: OPS together with Optimal Power Synergy India have completed a new solar PV, micro-grid system in the Andaman Islands.
The connected power sources include 75kWp of solar PV, a 240V, 2400Ah battery bank and 2 diesel generator sets rated to 30kVA each.
OPS India have supplied two proprietary technology parallel inverter modules rated at 20kVA each, with a Station Control Module and other balance of system, power electronic components.
The Station Control Module employs specialised control software and will take suitable actions, based on inbuilt intelligence, to ensure reliable and optimum operation of the hybrid power plant.
The Station Controller also provides remote monitoring of various system parameters such as battery status, loads, diesel genset operation.
This micro-grid system provides excellent power reliability and redundancy as well as significant diesel fuel savings.

The islanded system combines PV, a high-voltage DC battery bank, redundant parallel inverter modules, and two diesel generator sets. The Station Control Module monitors and dispatches the battery, site load, and generators and supports remote monitoring.

The 2014 commissioning release describes one 75 kWp site with two 20 kVA inverters. OPS's later portfolio page describes eight 40 kWp systems totaling 320 kWp with eight 20 kVA inverters. These may represent different sites or portfolio phases, but the sources do not reconcile them, so the ratings are not combined.

The original source reports 240 V and 2,400 Ah, while the portfolio page reports 575 kWh. No allowable depth of discharge, inverter-limited output, load profile, or battery degradation data is public, so a defensible storage duration cannot be stated.

No post-commissioning source found provides the exact island/site name, current operator and maintenance contractor, equipment replacements, operational availability, load served, generation by source, fuel savings, or present condition. A supplier portfolio entry and a 2024 project-listing are insufficient to prove current operation.

annobon-island.summary: Annobón's island electrification was announced in 2014 as a 5 MW solar microgrid for roughly 5,000 residents and was recorded by a New Jersey government storage study as commissioned in 2015 with 5 MW of PV, 5 MW/10 MWh of GE Durathon sodium-nickel-chloride batteries, 5 MW of diesel generation, twenty Princeton Power BIGI-250 converters, and Princeton EMOS controls. Later evidence does not describe a single stable configuration: Equatorial Guinea's 2024 budget continued to fund a 5 MVA Annobón solar-electrification line, while a cable supplier claims a 4.2 MWp/2.8 MWh hybrid configuration was supplied in 2023 and served 85% of demand by the second quarter of 2024.
annobon-island.status: The original project was recorded as commissioned in 2015, but present configuration and availability are not independently established. A 2024 enacted-budget line shows continued spending on 5 MVA solar electrification through 2025-2026. GERITEL's April 2026 retrospective claims that a 4.2 MWp/2.8 MWh system supplied in 2023 covered 85% of residents' demand by Q2 2024. Public evidence does not establish whether the later work was a replacement, rehabilitation, extension, or restatement of the 2015 5 MW/10 MWh system, so July 2026 operating status remains unverified.

equipment.detail: Sodium-nickel-chloride high-temperature storage rated 5 MW/10 MWh in the commissioned-system record. GE stopped Durathon production in 2015 after judging it not cost-competitive, and transferred the technology to a joint venture in 2017; no source reviewed identifies Annobón replacement cells or current capacity.

Supervisory controller for dispatch of the solar, storage, diesel, and distribution system.
Engineering services, switchgear assemblies, circuit protection, and transformers with embedded communications.
Supplier claims 18,500 m of UV- and salt-resistant PV cable for more than 6,000 modules in the later 4.2 MWp configuration.

Supplier claims 12,800 m of H07V-K battery cable for more than 200 battery modules, 6,200 m of YJV 3x70 mm² low-voltage backbone, 6.35 kV armored XLPE medium-voltage cable, and 9,600 m of KVV/KVVP control cable.
Reviewed sources do not identify the original PV module make/model, battery unit count, diesel-generator make/model, or the manufacturer/model/chemistry of the 2023 supplier-claimed BESS.

technicalDetails.detail: The original design combined PV, a two-hour battery plant, diesel generation, three-port converters, and EMOS controls in an island-wide microgrid. GE described storage as balancing intermittent production and demand while operating in tropical heat without air conditioning.

A 2019 New Jersey Board of Public Utilities storage analysis records the 5 MW PV and 5 MW/10 MWh sodium-nickel-chloride system as commissioned in 2015; Wise Power separately describes the 5 MW plant as installed.

The Equatorial Guinea government announcement said approximately twenty national technicians would be trained to operate and maintain the plant. No follow-up source located verifies completion or identifies the present O&M organization.

Princeton's 2015 presentation cited an installed cost of approximately $3.50/W and an electricity cost of approximately $0.25-$0.35/kWh including fuel. These were project presentation figures, not a later audited tariff or levelized-cost result.

Equatorial Guinea's 2024 enacted budget lists the 5 MVA Annobón solar-electrification project at CFA 23.155 billion total, with CFA 13.829 billion paid through June 2023, CFA 2.163 billion budgeted for 2024, and CFA 7.163 billion remaining for 2025-2026. The line proves continued public investment, but does not by itself establish whether generation equipment was incomplete, being rehabilitated, or being expanded.

GERITEL says an unnamed European EPC ordered tropicalized cabling in the 2023 dry season for a 4.2 MWp PV/2.8 MWh BESS hybrid, shipped via Bata. It claims that by Q2 2024 the microgrid met 85% of residents' demand, allowed diesel generators to remain off for long periods, supported continuous hospital refrigeration, and extended night classes to 9 p.m. These retrospective supplier claims were not independently corroborated in the sources reviewed.

The 2015 commissioned-system record gives 5 MW PV and 5 MW/10 MWh storage; the 2024 budget uses 5 MVA; GERITEL's later account gives 4.2 MWp/2.8 MWh. No public source reconciles these as design revision, rehabilitation, replacement, extension, or separate phase, so they remain dated configurations rather than a summed plant rating.

No independently verified operating data were found for 2015-2023 or after Q2 2024, including availability, generation by source, fuel use, battery health, current output, outages, or O&M responsibility. The effect of discontinued GE Durathon production and the identity of any replacement storage are also unknown.

ascension-island-microgrid.summary: This record is the U.S. Department of the Air Force power system at Ascension Auxiliary Airfield (AAAF), not the separate Ascension Island Government utility roadmap. The airfield's six-turbine, 2.7 MW wind fleet had become non-functional by 2021, leaving the installation dependent on shipped diesel. A subsequent ERCIP project replaced it with one EWT DW54 rated 900 kW, a 1 MW battery energy system, upgraded SCADA, and integration with the existing diesel plant and electrical distribution system.
ascension-island-microgrid.status: The replacement wind-battery system was installed and described by EWT as operating successfully in a 2024 case study. The federal contract record is marked complete, but its end date moved from the original 2022-2023 window to 31 October 2025 and its value rose from $11.307 million to $12.271 million. No reviewed government or independently measured record publishes final acceptance, current availability, annual wind production, battery energy capacity, or realized fuel savings through 20 July 2026; the Air Force's 15%-20% reduction was a pre-construction forecast, not an operating result.

equipment.detail: The contract includes a SCADA upgrade, but the reviewed Air Force release does not name the controller or SCADA vendor.

Direct-drive turbine rated 900 kW for this project, with a 54 m rotor and IEC IIIA design. The brochure lists generic 40 m, 50 m, and 75 m hub-height options but does not identify which height was installed at Ascension.
1 MW system integrated with wind, diesel, distribution, and SCADA. The battery manufacturer, model, chemistry, and MWh capacity are not public in the reviewed records.
Upgraded SCADA was part of the ERCIP scope for seamless monitoring and operation of the combined utility generation system; supplier and product were not disclosed.
Two 1.6 MW containerized CEMIRT Emergency Power System units were installed in 2012 and tied into the fuel and protection systems. Their present availability is not documented.

technicalDetails.detail: The Air Force described diesel as primary generation, wind as supplemental generation, and the BESS as instantaneous backup if an existing diesel generator fails. The storage and upgraded SCADA connect to the existing generation and distribution system; EWT also says excess wind is stored for low-wind periods.

Wind phase one entered service in 1996 and phase two in September 2003. By August 2021 all six turbines were non-functional and the airfield was 100% diesel-dependent. The replacement contract was awarded in July 2021; EWT documented the installed DW54 and battery in 2024; the contract record later showed completion with an October 2025 end date.

For the superseded four-turbine phase one, the Air Force reported more than 20,000 MWh cumulative production, 97% availability, and $2.5 million savings by 2004. Phase-two annual savings of more than 700,000 gallons and $1 million were projections, not the performance of the current system.

Ricardo separately modeled an optimal wind-solar-battery mix capable of more than 85% renewables for the Ascension Island Government from December 2021 to March 2022. That was an island-government roadmap and grant-planning exercise, not an as-built rating or outcome for the U.S. airfield ERCIP project.

Public evidence through 20 July 2026 does not disclose final government acceptance, current measured output or renewable share, actual fuel savings, current diesel capacity, BESS energy capacity/duration/chemistry, or SCADA and battery suppliers. EWT's qualitative claims of reliability and reduced emissions are vendor claims without quantified operating data.

ausnet-grid-energy-storage-system.summary: AusNet's Grid Energy Storage System (GESS) is a portable 1 MW/1 MWh lithium-ion battery and 1 MVA diesel hybrid, originally commissioned at Thomastown Terminal Station in Melbourne for 22 kV network trials. AusNet physically relocated it in 2020 and commissioned it at Mallacoota in 2021 as the Mallacoota Area Grid Storage (MAGS), where it islands the remote township during feeder outages. The original ABB-led system uses Samsung SDI batteries, an ABB PowerStore PCS100 inverter, Microgrid Plus controls, and containerized switchgear/protection.
ausnet-grid-energy-storage-system.status: Operating at Mallacoota at the latest owner update. MAGS suffered repeated technical issues in 2023 and again in February-May 2025, including waits for Samsung parts, but AusNet reported it back in service on 14 July 2025. It supplied more than 73 hours of backup during outages from September 2024 through April 2025, including a 13-hour-10-minute event on 17 April. No owner update after July 2025 was found by 20 July 2026, so present-day availability beyond that dated statement is not independently established.

specifications.value: 1MW Lithium Ion

specifications.value: 1 MW / 1 MWh lithium-ion, a nominal one-hour system at rated real power; ABB calls it a 1C system with symmetric charge/discharge capability of plus or minus 1 MW.

specifications.label: Diesel generation

specifications.label: Mallacoota endurance example
specifications.value: AusNet said the 1 MWh battery could serve 1,000 average homes for about two hours and the hybrid could supply the town for up to one day with the generator. This load example does not change the battery's nominal 1 MW/1 MWh rating.

specifications.label: Energy sources at Mallacoota
specifications.value: Battery charged from the grid, diesel generator, and customer rooftop solar present on the islanded local network. The system itself has no dedicated utility-scale renewable generator and no verified annual renewable-energy share.

specifications.label: Mallacoota project contribution
specifications.value: $7.5 million contributed by AusNet; the reviewed sources do not provide a full final project cost or separate relocation cost.

equipment.detail: 1 MWh, 1C lithium-ion BESS in four self-contained shipping containers with HVAC and fire suppression, capable of plus or minus 1 MW. AusNet's public rating is 1 MWh; no cell model or chemistry subtype is published.
IGBT four-quadrant inverter on a 1,000 V DC bus, rated plus or minus 1,372 kVA; operates as a voltage-source synthetic generator and STATCOM-like resource.

Monitors power and energy flows, maintains spinning reserve, coordinates generator/battery load sharing, transitions between grid-connected and island modes, and links by RTU to AusNet's control system.
Three intelligent electronic devices protect and monitor the system; protection groups change automatically as sources enter and leave service.
Sends signals to Microgrid Plus so PowerStore and the generator match the upstream network voltage and frequency for reconnection.
Containerized 1 MVA backup generator. The reviewed manufacturer/owner sources do not establish its manufacturer, exact model, or real-power rating.

technicalDetails.detail: 1 MW / 1 MWh Lithium Ion for Peak Lopping, Feeder Islanding, Voltage Regulation, and Phase Balancing

AusNet initiated the two-year trial in 2013 and had hardware installed at Thomastown by the end of 2014. Tests covered peak lopping, power-factor correction, voltage and harmonic support, phase balance, and islanded transitions. AusNet selected the diesel hybrid because additional diesel endurance cost less than expanding battery capacity.

AusNet developed the relocation concept in 2017, carried out enhancement and reliability work with ABB, physically moved the GESS from Thomastown to Mallacoota in 2020, began recommissioning that December, and announced the completed Mallacoota installation in 2021.

In island mode, PowerStore supplies the feeder and establishes voltage/frequency so the diesel generator can synchronize. Battery and generator then share load; the generator can increase output as battery state of charge falls. Grid-connected services include charging at low demand, peak lopping, reactive-power control, and voltage droop.

Rooftop solar remains connected behind the islanded town load. AusNet warned in September 2023 that high solar export while the MAGS generator was supplying the town could create excess reverse flow and functional issues; operators and an automatic control system adjust power flow accordingly.

Technical failures prevented backup during some 2023 and February-May 2025 outages. AusNet added a local registered electrical contractor, upgraded generator controls, installed automatic switches, and implemented remote start if automatic start failed, which could take up to 30 minutes. Some repairs depended on Samsung parts.

AusNet's update records more than 73 hours of backup from September 2024 through April 2025 across ten listed events. In April 2025 it supported five transient outages and one sustained outage, with the sustained event lasting 13 hours 10 minutes.

The original 1 MW/1 MWh Thomastown GESS is now MAGS at Mallacoota. It is not AusNet's separate proposed 300 MW/600 MWh Thomastown BESS, whose planning permit was approved and whose construction was intended to start in late 2026.

No post-July-2025 owner update, current availability factor, annual energy throughput, diesel use, rooftop-solar share, battery degradation/capacity test, or complete replacement history was found through 20 July 2026.

babylon-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: The Village of Babylon project was a NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility study for a community microgrid serving critical municipal, school, shelter, and relief facilities on Long Island. Ameresco's August 2016 final concept paired two 500 kW natural-gas engine-generators with 275 kW of solar PV, a dedicated 13.2 kV microgrid feeder, automated switching, controls, and efficiency upgrades. It was never an operating microgrid: Ameresco later stated that it did not advance to Stage 2 because the economics were poor.
babylon-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Feasibility completed on 2 August 2016; not advanced to NY Prize Stage 2 and not built. The $4.635 million concept had a 0.5 benefit-cost ratio without assumed major outages, and Ameresco estimated an approximately $2.1 million monetizable-revenue shortfall. No procurement, construction, commissioning, or later revival was found through 20 July 2026. All equipment and performance values below are study-stage designs or estimates, not as-built assets.

specifications.value: NA

specifications.label: Conceptual solar PV
specifications.value: 275 kW total: 50 kW at Village Hall/Department of Public Works and 225 kW at Babylon Junior-Senior High School; no module or inverter supplier was selected.

specifications.value: NYSERDA's initial Stage 1 winner description mentioned energy storage and fuel cells, but Ameresco's final August 2016 design included neither a BESS nor a fuel cell. No storage power, energy, duration, chemistry, or vendor should be assigned to the final concept.

specifications.label: Critical-facility load
specifications.value: Combined normal peak estimated at about 400-500 kW; modeled post-efficiency peaks were approximately 350 kW in winter and 310 kW in summer.

specifications.label: Distribution concept
specifications.value: Dedicated 13.2 kV microgrid distribution using approximately 2,500 feet of 15 kV cable, six automatic transfer/open switches, one 1.5 MVA 480 V/13.7 kV transformer, and two 13.2 kV breakers.

specifications.label: Capital-cost estimate
specifications.value: $4,635,436 including contingency and engineering; major subtotals included $1.943 million for generation/switchgear, $264,500 for distribution, $915,000 for renewables and controls, and $585,749 for efficiency measures.

specifications.label: Benefit-cost results
specifications.value: At a 7% discount rate over 20 years, the no-major-outage case had $2.17 million benefits, negative $2.04 million net benefits, and a 0.5 ratio. Assuming an average three-day major outage every year raised the ratio to 1.0 and net benefits to only $15,200.

equipment.detail: Two continuous-duty 500 kW, 480 V units with black-start starter batteries and exhaust treatment were budgeted. No manufacturer or model was procured or specified.
Two arrays totaling 275 kW were budgeted at municipal and school properties. No module, inverter, mounting, or EPC vendor was selected.

The report uses this product designation for the proposed controller, with CPU, HMI/SCADA, 100 Mbps Ethernet, automatic island/reconnect logic, generation dispatch, and load shedding. It does not identify a manufacturer, and the system was not purchased.

technicalDetails.detail: The proposed microgrid would use conventional generation as well as solar, energy storage, and fuel cells.
Conventional generation as well as solar, energy storage, and fuel cells

The final study covered Village Hall/Fire/EMS, Department of Public Works, Babylon Junior-Senior High School, American Legion Post 94, and the two-building St. Joseph's School complex. These loads were selected for emergency response, shelter, and relief roles demonstrated during Superstorm Sandy.

During an outage the design would isolate the 13.2 kV feeder, black-start both 500 kW generators, energize the feeder, add facilities in a controlled sequence, and then connect PV. Controls could stop one generator as load and PV conditions allowed and later synchronize/reconnect to PSEG-LI.

The conceptual MCS would automatically island and reconnect, optimize generator dispatch and load shedding, provide local HMI/SCADA, and use Ethernet with optional DNP3 and Modbus TCP/IP/fiber communications plus GPS time synchronization. Generic references to storage in the controls section do not establish a BESS in the final equipment schedule.

The Stage 1 award sheet initially described conventional generation, solar, storage, and fuel cells and listed MTA, NYSDOT, Suffolk County, and the Town of Babylon among partners. The final study narrowed the facilities and settled on natural-gas generators plus PV without storage or fuel cells; it does not document construction roles for those initial partners.

The study said ownership, operating agreements, customer contracts, and financing still had to be formed. Ameresco's later presentation reported about $4.9 million NPV cost, $4.3 million broad societal benefits, only $2.8 million monetizable revenue, and a roughly $2.1 million shortfall, then stated that the project did not move to Stage 2 because of poor economics.

No evidence of Stage 2 award, detailed design, financing close, equipment procurement, construction, or operation was found through 20 July 2026. Consequently, all vendors other than the named study participants and all equipment manufacturers remain unknown.

baega-island.summary: Baega Island is the Baekado energy-self-sufficient-island project on Baekado (Korean: 백아도; also romanized Baega-do, Baegado, or Paega-do) in Baega-ri, Deokjeok-myeon, Ongjin County, Incheon. Commissioned in January 2015, it combined 250 kW of PV, four 10 kW wind turbines, about 1.1-1.125 MWh of lithium-ion storage, an energy-management system, and three existing 75 kW diesel generators. The public promise of a 100% clean/carbon-zero island was not achieved: a National Assembly Budget Office analysis found a 59% renewable/41% diesel energy mix in 2016.
baega-island.status: Historically commissioned, but current operation is unverified. Government records show completion in January 2015 and 2016 production of about 270,000 kWh renewable plus 190,000 kWh diesel. The wind component performed especially poorly: a 2019 KIER/Pusan National University study reported measured mean wind speed of 2.32 m/s and capacity factor of only 1.08%, citing a 2017 audit recommendation to relocate the turbines. No public evidence found through 20 July 2026 confirms that relocation occurred or establishes present PV, BESS, wind, EMS, or diesel availability; the record therefore should not be labeled currently 100% renewable or fully operational without qualification.

equipment.detail: Four 10 kW turbines on 12 m hubs. The 2019 site study attributes the installed units to Raeun Tech; the Korean Energy Agency's current certification record for the same model lists Geumpung, so the later certificate holder should not be retroactively substituted for the installed-project attribution.
About 1.1-1.125 MWh coupled through bidirectional conversion and EMS; public records reviewed do not identify the battery/PCS manufacturer, model, power rating, or current state of health.
250 kW array. Manufacturer and component models are not disclosed in the reviewed sources.
EMS and power-conversion equipment coordinate renewable generation, storage, and diesel backup; supplier and product are not disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: The local island name is 백아도 (Baekado). English sources variously use Baega, Baega-do, Baegado, or Paega-do. It is not Gasado/Gasa Island, a separate KEPCO microgrid with different ratings and participants.

The system was designed to prioritize PV and wind, use the lithium-ion ESS to balance production and load, and run existing diesel generation when renewable energy was insufficient. Diesel therefore remained physically and operationally part of the microgrid despite the carbon-zero branding.

Construction began in 2014 under the convergence-support program. The National Assembly analysis records commissioning in January 2015, and Incheon planning material described the completed system as in operation after 2014 work.

The 2016 renewable generation of roughly 270,000 kWh was only 73% of the 380,000 kWh plan, requiring 190,000 kWh of diesel output. The National Assembly Budget Office concluded that the promised 100% clean-energy supply had not been achieved.

The four turbines were installed in a coastal basin shielded on three sides by 70-130 m terrain. Tower height was reduced from the original 18 m design to 12 m to limit turbine shadow on the PV array. The 2017 audit found that field wind measurements were omitted and a non-specialist performed the site assessment, and recommended relocation.

The 2019 NWP-CFD study modeled a stronger hilltop location and estimated a 23.1% capacity factor there, compared with 4.2% modeled at the current site and 1.08% in reported operating data. This was an analytical relocation case, not evidence that the turbines were moved.

No reviewed public source after the 2019 wind study confirms turbine relocation, replacement, decommissioning, battery refurbishment, EMS upgrades, current generation mix, or component availability. The most defensible status is historically commissioned with documented underperformance, present operation unknown.

baikampady-mangalore-microgrid.summary: SELCO India commissioned a small direct-current solar microgrid in April 2014 for a 75-home informal labor colony in the Baikampady industrial estate north of Mangalore. The documented design combined 1.2 kWp of PV, a nominal 19.2 kWh battery bank, grid-assisted charging, and 96 VDC distribution for efficient lighting, phone charging, three fans, and one television. Canara Bank financed the system through a loan to the property owner, who collected service payments with rent. Public evidence located in this review documents implementation through 2015 but not the system's later operating condition.
baikampady-mangalore-microgrid.status: Commissioned in April 2014 and documented as implemented in a 2015 SELCO site summary. No reliable independent post-2015 operating or decommissioning evidence was found through July 2026, so present operating status is unknown.

The microgrid used 96 VDC distribution and prioritized solar, then battery, then grid-assisted charging. This avoided household AC inverters for the small DC loads.

Because a conventional grid extension to the informal settlement was impractical, the property owner borrowed from Canara Bank, owned the shared plant, and recovered the monthly payment through rent/service collections, displacing kerosene lighting.
SELCO documented a local data logger but no remote monitoring connection.

banggi-island.summary: Sabah Electricity's Banggi Island station is a solar-battery-diesel hybrid supplying homes, schools, and businesses on the island. Current owner documentation gives 1,200 kWp of PV, a 720-cell gel battery bank, and eight diesel generators ranging from 200 kW to 1,000 kW against an approximately 950 kW served load. A Leonics engineering presentation for the project records a 1,200 kWp array, 2,075 kW of inverter capacity, a 2,880 kWh main battery bank, and 1,650 kVA of diesel capacity. The station has received battery, inverter, and diesel upgrades and remains in service, but no source found provides a current annual renewable-energy percentage.
banggi-island.status: Operating under Sabah Electricity as of the owner's current ESG account reviewed in July 2026. That account says battery and inverter upgrades and added diesel capacity have sustained performance. Commissioning dates conflict by phase/source: TNB describes commissioning in 2013, while Leonics records commercial operation on 25 January 2014 and operation since February 2014.

Hybrid Control and Communication Unit (HCCU) coordinating PV, batteries, and diesel generation.

technicalDetails.detail: Sample application of PV – Wind Turbine- Diesel Generator Hybrid Minigrid System

The vendor diagrams show a mixed AC/DC-coupled hybrid: PV grid inverters feed the AC bus, MPPT units charge a 480 VDC battery system, bidirectional inverters form/support the island grid, and diesel generators provide backup and firm capacity.

TNB's current account says the plant was commissioned in 2013. Leonics' project documentation instead gives 25 January 2014 as COD and describes operation since February 2014; these may reflect construction, handover, or phased commercial-operation milestones.

The current owner says battery and inverter upgrades have sustained performance and additional diesel capacity has been installed for high demand. It does not identify the replacement vendors, models, dates, or energy capacity.

A Leonics slide labels 4.93 MW by arithmetically summing PV nameplate, inverter capacity, and diesel capacity. That figure is not a valid independent generating-capacity rating and is not used here as plant output.

bario.summary: The Bario Central Solar Hybrid Power System supplies the remote Bario Highlands with an off-grid combination of PV, battery storage, and diesel backup. Sarawak Energy records construction beginning in 2010, initial commissioning in September 2015, transfer to its operation in June 2016, and an October 2016 official launch. The launch-era rating was 887 kW and at least 70% of generated energy was expected from solar. Vendor documents preserve design changes around 885.84-906 kWp of PV, 3,860 kWh of battery storage, and varying inverter/diesel totals. Sarawak's current Rural Electrification Masterplan describes a 998 kW station serving more than 400 households and proposes a future 33 kV grid interconnection.
bario.status: Operating as an off-grid solar-battery-diesel station under Sarawak Energy in the current Rural Electrification Masterplan reviewed in July 2026. The plan proposes interconnecting Bario to the state grid via an approximately 60 km Ba'kelalan-Bario line and 33 kV substation by 2030; that connection is not yet documented as complete.

equipment.detail: Early design diagrams show four 125 kW AC-coupled grid-inverter blocks; the slide's printed aggregate contains an arithmetic inconsistency, so the component count and ratings are retained without adopting its total.
Three 200 kW bidirectional units (600 kW total) connected to the 480 VDC, 3,860 kWh battery bank.
Eight 70 kW MPPT blocks are depicted for the 483.84 kWp DC-coupled PV section. One text extraction renders the multiplier incorrectly; eight units are consistent with the displayed 560 kW total.
Hybrid control and communications coordinate AC-coupled PV, DC-coupled PV/battery, and diesel generation.

technicalDetails.detail: Published engineering analysis describes a mixed-bus system: PV and batteries feed a DC bus through converters while diesel generators feed the AC bus. In the modeled load-following logic, PV serves load and charges storage; the battery discharges to its minimum state of charge before diesel is dispatched.
Construction began in 2010, first commissioning occurred in September 2015, Sarawak Energy accepted operation in June 2016, and the official launch was held on 25 October 2016.

The central plant initially covered nine villages. Two extension villages were expected to lift the central-plant reach above 300 homes, while five other small nearby settlements were assigned separate stand-alone hybrid systems and should not be counted as central-plant capacity.

Sarawak's Rural Electrification Masterplan proposes a 33 kV substation and roughly 60 km covered-conductor link from Ba'kelalan, interconnecting rather than simply replacing the hybrid station under the 2030 plan.

The 887 kW launch rating, 885.84/906 kWp vendor PV values, and 998 kW current masterplan value belong to different dated descriptions. They are retained separately rather than normalized into a single undocumented capacity.

baron-techno-park.summary: Baron Techno Park is an off-grid renewable-energy research and demonstration site at Parang Racuk, Gunungkidul. Its documented hybrid plant combined 36 kW of PV, two wind turbines totaling 15 kW, nominal 25 kW diesel and 25 kW biodiesel generators, a battery bank reported as 288 kVAh/1,200 Ah, and a 25 kVA hybrid controller. The available plant changed over time: a 2017 study said only 24 kW of PV and 5 kW of wind were operating, while a 2023 engineering paper describes a current 36 kW PV/5 kW wind/20 kWh battery/25 kW inverter configuration and reports that the Fortis wind turbine had been stopped by a 2019 malfunction.
baron-techno-park.status: Continues to be documented as an off-grid research/test microgrid. The 5 kW Fortis wind subsystem had been out of service since a 2019 malfunction and was planned for reinstallation in the 2023 paper; no later public confirmation of its return to service was found through July 2026. PV/battery operation is described in 2023, but a recent measured generation mix was not located.

equipment.detail: Published rating is 25 kVA; manufacturer and model were not disclosed in the reviewed sources.
5 kW DC-output wind turbine connected through the site's main control room and hybrid inverter; reported stopped since 2019 because of malfunction.

Three nominal 14.4 kWp module groupings are described by component count (120 x 120 Wp each), although the installed project rating is consistently reported as 36 kW; this published arithmetic inconsistency is not silently resolved.
25 kVA-class unit in the main control room, integrating PV, wind, storage, dispatchable generation, and data acquisition.

The 2017 profile describes two arrays, eight strings per array and 15 batteries per string, while also printing 120 cells; the count statements are internally inconsistent. A 2023 paper instead characterizes the active configuration as 20 kWh.

technicalDetails.detail: PV, wind, biodiesel/diesel, battery storage, and inverter resources connect through an off-grid hybrid controller with data acquisition. The site is primarily a demonstration and research platform rather than a utility-scale supply plant.
The 2017 performance study says obstacles left only 24 kW of the 36 kW PV inventory and 5 kW of the 15 kW wind inventory operating at the study time.
The Fortis 5 kW turbine stopped operating in 2019 due to malfunction. The 2023 paper says reinstallation was planned in the near future, but the review found no later commissioning confirmation.
A 2015 site account says the biodiesel demonstration shifted from unsuccessful jatropha feedstock toward used cooking oil; the installation was a pilot/research line, not commercial mass production.
Large annual generation and energy-mix figures in the 2017 paper are HOMER-model results, not metered operating production, and are therefore not presented as an observed energy mix here.

blue-lake-rancheria-microgrid.summary: Blue Lake Rancheria's main community microgrid has operated since 2017 with a 420 kW SolarWorld/SMA PV plant, a 500 kW/950 kWh Tesla Powerpack battery system through two Dynapower inverters, a Siemens Spectrum Power microgrid controller, and a 1 MW Cummins diesel generator. It has islanded through grid outages and supported the surrounding community during disasters. A second Play Station 777 microgrid entered service in 2020, a new Tesla Megapack installation was commissioned in 2024 without a published capacity, and a $5 million 2025 California Energy Commission award is funding a nested 12 kV Microgrid 2.0 expansion.
blue-lake-rancheria-microgrid.status: The 2017 main microgrid and 2020 Play Station 777 microgrid are operational. The tribe reports commissioning an additional Tesla Megapack installation in 2024, but does not disclose its power or energy capacity. The nested Microgrid 2.0 expansion was funded in June 2025 and remained an active demonstration/expansion effort in 2026; it should not yet be counted as commissioned capacity.

equipment.detail: Coordinates the solar, storage, generator, loads, and islanded operation.
500 kW / 950 kWh battery energy storage system.
420 kW community-scale array; the owner team lists SunPower and REC Solar among project vendors.
1,548 modules forming the 420 kW array.
Fourteen 30 kW units for the original PV plant.
Ten Powerpacks, totaling 500 kW/950 kWh in the original commissioned system.
Two 250 kW power-conversion systems.
Supervisory microgrid management, dispatch, islanding, and power-flow control.
Building-load monitoring and automated load shedding coordinated with the microgrid controller.
Protection and interconnection relay at the utility interface.
1 MW diesel generator for prolonged island operation and resilience.

technicalDetails.detail: Biomass-gasification-fuel cell system that turns biomass into electricity.
The 175 kilowatt (kW) system is fueled by biomass (plant-based biological material) that has been reduced to sawdust size.

The original project finished on schedule with less than 1.5% cost overrun. It saved approximately $160,000 in 2017 and was expected to save about $200,000 annually thereafter while avoiding 159 metric tons CO2e in 2017 (about 175 tons expected annually).

The main system islanded seamlessly during a 2017 outage. Both tribal microgrids remained operational during a three-day 2019 utility outage, and the tribe reports supporting thousands of people after the December 2022 magnitude-6.4 earthquake.

A $5 million CEC award announced 11 June 2025 funds advanced 12 kV nested controls/protection, circuit reconfiguration, and added solar/storage across three facility microgrids, with PG&E invited to testing. Equipment vendors and final capacities were not fixed in the public scope and are not assigned here.
The nested project is intended to inform replication along the approximately 142-mile Hoopa 1101 feeder serving Hoopa Valley, Yurok, and Karuk communities.

The CEC final report for the commissioned 2017 system identifies PV, BESS, grid, and a diesel generator. It does not include the 175 kW biomass/fuel-cell concept found in older planning material; that concept should not be counted as installed main-microgrid capacity.
The tribe hosted an operating-microgrid tour in April 2026 and described ongoing TERAS and nested-microgrid expansion work, providing current evidence that the resilience program remains active.

bonaire-microgrid.summary: Bonaire's island power system is an operating hybrid of 11.1 MW of wind, 5.3 MW of solar, 28.5 MW of thermal generation, and a battery fleet currently described by ContourGlobal as 14 MW. The original 2010 renewable build used twelve Enercon E-44/900 turbines, one E-33/330 turbine, MAN diesel engines, and a 3 MW Saft nickel-cadmium battery. Wärtsilä added a 6 MW/6 MWh battery and GEMS controls in 2019, and ContourGlobal added a further battery and the 5.3 MW solar plant in 2023. The system remains the island's sole bulk supply to distributor WEB, although 2024 demand growth, extreme heat, and cable damage caused outages and prompted capacity and grid upgrades.
bonaire-microgrid.status: Operating under ContourGlobal, with WEB distributing electricity. Current owner figures are 28.5 MW thermal, 11.1 MW wind, 14 MW BESS power, and 5.3 MW solar. WEB reported 2024 reliability problems and added 5 MW of production capacity while upgrading controls and transmission. Wind repowering/expansion is planned, but the publicly stated 60% renewable target was not verified as achieved and no current audited annual energy mix was found.

One 330 kW unit at Sorobon.
3 MW system in three containers at 640 V and 1,320 Ah; designed for more than two minutes of immediate support while diesel generation starts and to absorb power while held near 90% state of charge.

Controls the hybrid thermal, wind, and storage fleet and supports fast frequency response and renewable integration.

technicalDetails.detail: The energy storage system is also designed to typical grid fluctuations.
The battery array is normally held at 90% state-of-charge so it can be brought on line as an artificial load when necessary.
Biodiesel: (5) 2.85 MW MAN diesel engines
Wind: (11) 1 MW Enercon wind turbines

Wind and solar are dispatched with battery storage and thermal generators on a single island grid. The batteries provide fast frequency/power-quality support, absorb excess renewable output, and bridge the interval while diesel units start.

Wärtsilä reports that the 6 MW/6 MWh addition reduced CO2 emissions by 8%, fossil-fuel use by 5%, eliminated renewable curtailment, and increased annual wind utilization by 6-8%. Load-rejection tests demonstrated continued operation after loss of wind, engines, or load without customer shedding.

ContourGlobal's current report describes the additional battery as commercially operating in April 2023 and the 5.3 MW PV plant later in 2023. Local reporting places battery commissioning in July and solar commissioning in September; the difference likely reflects commercial-operation versus local handover milestones.

WEB attributed recent outages to an unexpected 15-20% demand increase, extreme heat, and cable damage. It reported adding 5 MW of production capacity, digitizing controls, and advancing transmission upgrades with a January 2025 target.

ContourGlobal says it is pursuing wind repowering that would more than double wind capacity. WEB also identifies wind-farm expansion in its 2024-2030 transition planning; no completed repower rating was located.

The current 14 MW BESS figure is a power rating. It must not be converted to 14 MWh or simply added to historical energy ratings because the owner does not publish the current aggregate duration/energy capacity.

bornholm-island.summary: Bornholm is a live island power system and long-running smart-grid test platform connected to Sweden by one subsea cable. EcoGrid EU enrolled roughly 2,000 households in 2011-2015, and EcoGrid 2.0 concluded in 2019 after remotely coordinating electric heating in 800 homes. Under normal conditions BEOF says its own local generation is entirely wind, solar, biomass, and biogas, while the island imports Nordic electricity when local renewable output is insufficient and starts Rønne thermal units during cable outages. BEOF announced a 10 MW Hitachi Energy battery for installation in the old Rønne coal plant in 2026, but no public commissioning confirmation was found by this review.
bornholm-island.status: EcoGrid EU and EcoGrid 2.0 are complete demonstrations; Bornholm remains an operating island grid and active Test Island Bornholm platform. BEOF and Energinet signed a May 2026 reserve-supply agreement for 2027-2031. A 10 MW Hitachi Energy battery was announced for 2026 installation but is not counted as commissioned absent confirmation. The separate 3 GW Bornholm Energy Island offshore project was revived by a January 2026 Danish-German agreement and remains future infrastructure.

Diesel, steam, CHP, wind, biogas
Bornholm is connected to the Swedish/Nordic grid by a single subsea cable. When that connection is unavailable, BEOF maintains island-operation capability using local reserve plant.

The 2016-2019 demonstration remotely coordinated heat pumps and electric heaters in 800 homes to test flexible demand against grid and market needs. The earlier target of 1,000 devices/households should not replace the realized 800-household figure.
BEOF reports more than ten years as a full-scale test island, a portfolio valued around DKK650 million, and more than EUR100 million of project activity handled.

The May 2026 BEOF-Energinet agreement requires BEOF to maintain reserve generation and response for 2027-2031 when the Sweden cable is unavailable, replacing the arrangement that runs through the end of 2026.
BEOF announced the 10 MW battery on 13 March 2025 for installation during 2026. No public commissioning confirmation was found by 20 July 2026, so it remains classified as announced/in installation.

The January 2026 Danish-German agreement revived the separate 3 GW Bornholm Energy Island offshore transmission project. It is future interconnector/offshore-wind infrastructure and must not be added to the current island microgrid fleet.

borrego-microgrid.summary: SDG&E's Borrego Springs community microgrid serves an isolated desert feeder with extensive local solar, batteries, an ultracapacitor, diesel backup, and advanced islanding controls. The original system supported thousands of customers during major 2013 outages. A major Microgrid 3.0 upgrade replaces two 1.8 MW diesels with two 2.5 MW Tier IV Final units and adds six Tesla battery containers totaling 7 MW/14 MWh. Replacement generators were commissioned in March 2025, while June 2025 community minutes say the battery was still commissioning and local/remote control integration remained unfinished. DOE/NREL work nevertheless completed field validation of renewable-led black start and islanding using a grid-forming battery and PXiSE controller.
borrego-microgrid.status: Operating community microgrid in a major upgrade/integration phase. The two 2.5 MW replacement diesels were commissioned and functional by March 2025. The 7 MW/14 MWh Tesla BESS was still commissioning on 4 June 2025, with local controls targeted for September and remote integration by year-end; no later official completion confirmation was found through July 2026. A proposed 250 kW, 16-hour hydrogen resource also remains unverified as commissioned.

Coordinates batteries, ultracapacitor, diesel generation, switching, load management, and renewable-led black start in the later control architecture.
Six battery containers totaling 7 MW/14 MWh; installation began in August 2024 and commissioning was ongoing in June 2025.
Two 2.5 MW Tier IV Final units replacing the original two 1.8 MW machines; commissioned and functional by March 2025. The located sources do not identify the manufacturer/model.

•Home Energy Storage (HES)
• Six 4 kW/8 kWh units
• Two (2) 1.8 MW SDG&E Caterpillar Diesel Generators

The microgrid normally remains grid-connected/dormant while local solar exports. During daytime island operation it can serve the whole community; at night it prioritizes critical loads and can shed noncritical load according to available battery and fuel.
After an April 2013 windstorm the microgrid served 1,225 customers for about six hours. During a September 2013 storm that downed 20 poles, it served 1,060 customers for roughly 25 hours.

CEC's 2024 review records several successful planned outages but also failed islanding demonstrations caused by controller-communications and frequency-stabilization problems, showing that nameplate capacity alone did not guarantee successful transitions.

Community minutes report that both legacy generators failed during a 61-hour planned February 2023 microgrid operation, taking the system out of service. The replacement generators were commissioned by March 2025.

The DOE/NREL project completed modeling, controller/hardware-in-the-loop work, and field tests in which a grid-forming battery led black start and islanding instead of diesel. Synchrophasor and power-quality measurements verified the fast response, and the DERMS interface was replicated at other SDG&E sites.

SDG&E's 2024 fact sheet expected completion in Q2 2025, but 4 June 2025 minutes say batteries were still commissioning, local controls were targeted for September, and remote control-center integration for year-end. The later direct evidence supersedes the forecast.

The CPUC describes planned CAISO market participation for the upgraded assets; it also says the then-current microgrid did not provide resource adequacy. This is a plan, not verified operating revenue/service.

bosch-dc-microgrid-california-honda-faci.summary: California Energy Commission grant EPC-14-053 funded Bosch's 380 V direct-current building microgrid demonstration at an American Honda Motor Co. warehouse in Chino, California. The final installation coupled 286 kW of rooftop solar, a 180 kW/540 kWh lithium-ion battery, Bosch energy management, DC lighting, fan drives, and forklift charging on a common DC bus. CEC's 2024 retrospective says the system was removed after the grant because Bosch discontinued the commercial offering.
bosch-dc-microgrid-california-honda-faci.status: Demonstration completed in 2019 and subsequently uninstalled; CEC reported in 2024 that Bosch discontinued the DC Building Microgrid Platform offering.

technicalDetails.detail: The buildings solar PV arrays, DC ventalation system, DC lighting, and DC energy storage will form a microgrid on a 380-volt DC bus.
The building-scale system avoided repeated DC-to-AC-to-DC conversions by linking rooftop PV, battery storage, and compatible building loads on a 380 V DC bus while retaining utility service.

CEC reported 2-6% DC-load efficiency improvement, 7-10% greater solar utilization, and an 8% storage-efficiency improvement; the 2019 EPIC summary reported 3-5% overall efficiency improvement for lighting, ventilation, and forklift charging.
The system supported designated critical loads during outage testing and avoided use of the site's diesel backup generator.

CEC estimated roughly 30% lower total cost of ownership at commercial scale; the host facility manager estimated 45-50% lower maintenance cost, but CEC cautioned that project delays left limited baseline and operating data.

Flow-battery supplier failure, interconnection delays, a separately managed rooftop-solar project, and rate changes delayed the work. After the grant, Bosch discontinued the product offering and the demonstration equipment was uninstalled, so the project did not proceed to a commercial launch.
Current CEC and PNNL project records place the host warehouse in Chino, California, not Rancho Cordova.

brevoort-co-op-microgrid.summary: The Brevoort cooperative at 11 Fifth Avenue in Manhattan installed a 400 kW natural-gas combined-heat-and-power microgrid built from four 100 kW Tecogen InVerde modules. Commissioned in 2010, it normally operated in parallel with Con Edison under zero-export control and islanded for six days after Hurricane Sandy, serving electricity, heat, domestic hot water, elevators, and other building loads.
brevoort-co-op-microgrid.status: Commissioned in 2010 and documented operating through at least 2013; no reliable public source confirms the system's operating condition through 2026.

90-ton absorption chiller using recovered CHP heat
Tecogen inverter controls with CERTS autonomous power sharing and a utility-protection panel for isolation and reconnection

technicalDetails.detail: Cogeneration saves $300,000 / year
In normal grid-parallel operation the controls maintained at least 50 kW of utility import and prohibited export; typically three engines ran in winter and all four in summer.

UL 1741-certified CERTS logic let modules share load autonomously without a master controller. A utility-protection panel detected grid failure, opened the service breaker, and initiated an island restart.

The automatic utility breaker did not operate during Sandy, so the superintendent manually isolated the building and restarted the CHP. The building then ran islanded for six days, supporting residents and temporary guests with electricity, heat, water, and elevators.
The IEEE performance paper calculated as much as 92% total fuel utilization when all recoverable heat was used.
The IEEE analysis calculated $228,871 of 2013 energy savings on a 2007-cost basis and expected payback under five years; EPA reported approximately $300,000 annual realized savings.
This record is the Brevoort at 11 Fifth Avenue and should not be combined with the separate Brevoort East cooperative project, which public databases list with different CHP capacity.

brookhaven-microgrid-feasibility-study.summary: Brookhaven's Independence Hill Microgrid was a NY Prize Stage 1 feasibility design linking Brookhaven Town Hall and Sachem High School East on Long Island. The preferred concept combined two new natural-gas CHP plants, two existing 10 kW wind turbines, existing diesel backup, and a proposed battery over an underground interconnection. The study did not establish that the project was built.
brookhaven-microgrid-feasibility-study.status: Stage 1 feasibility study completed in 2016. The Independence Hill project is absent from NYSERDA's published complete list of Stage 2 awardees, indicating it did not advance in NY Prize; no construction or commissioning evidence was found through 2026.

technicalDetails.detail: Technology would include solar panels and two existing 10 kW wind turbines, fuel cells, battery storage, and/or microturbines powered by the onsite wastewater treatment plant.

The project would incorporate sophisticated monitoring software operated by Brookhaven National Laboratory that will maximize the cost-benefit of the electrical generation and storage capacity components of the microgrid.
Technology would include solar panels and two existing 10 kW wind turbines, fuel cells, battery storage, and/or microturbines powered by the onsite wastewater treatment plant.

The two facilities would be joined by an underground distribution cable and normally operate grid-connected without exporting power. Controls were intended to detect outages, island automatically, prioritize renewable generation, shed noncritical loads, black-start, and reconnect after utility restoration.
The study modeled annual CHP output of 5,608 MWh at Town Hall and 3,350 MWh at the school, plus 22 MWh per year from each wind turbine. Diesel generation was reserved for outage operation.

The preferred design was modeled to cover 98% of annual electric needs and assumed seven days of natural-gas availability during a major outage; these were feasibility assumptions, not measured results.
The study projected $810,352 in annual energy savings, approximately $140,000 annual operating and maintenance cost, and about a 15-year simple payback.

The feasibility model reduced annual carbon dioxide at both sites but increased NOx and particulate emissions under its uncontrolled natural-gas CHP assumptions, illustrating that the proposal was not an all-renewable design.
Tecumseh Elementary School was evaluated initially and then removed because the district planned to close it; the final study covered Town Hall and Sachem High School East only.

NYSERDA describes its published Stage 2 list as complete, and Independence Hill is not present. The conclusion that it stopped after Stage 1 is therefore a program-record inference rather than an explicit cancellation notice.

brooklyn-microgrid.summary: Brooklyn Microgrid was LO3 Energy's peer-to-peer local-energy trading pilot in Brooklyn, New York. Participants used custom meters and the Exergy blockchain platform to transact attributes associated with rooftop solar while electricity continued to flow over Con Edison's distribution system. A physical ten-block islandable microgrid was discussed, but public evidence documents a virtual marketplace pilot rather than a completed electrical island.
brooklyn-microgrid.status: Pilot no longer operating by 2025. Public activity is documented from the first 2016 trades through a 2019 regulatory-sandbox effort; the project site stopped updating in 2019 and LO3's site later disappeared.

technicalDetails.detail: Users could bid according to price, fuel source, and other energy attributes through an application. Early transactions represented local solar value while financial settlement and actual power delivery still depended on conventional systems and Con Edison wires.

The demonstrated system was a virtual peer-to-peer marketplace. Islanding a ten-block section to support a high school, medical facilities, and other critical loads was a future plan, not a documented completed capability.
New York rules did not generally permit non-utilities to sell electricity directly. The 2019 sandbox therefore focused on trading energy attributes while regulators evaluated a broader framework.

A 2025 Electrosuisse follow-up found the project had quietly ended: brooklyn.energy had not been updated since 2019, LO3's website was gone, and an early participant confirmed the marketplace was no longer operating. His solar array remained an ordinary Con Edison-interconnected system.
The first demonstration was also called TransActive Grid; later public materials used Brooklyn Microgrid and BMG.

burlington-dc-microgrid.summary: ARDA Power's industrial DC microgrid demonstration in Burlington, Ontario, used a bipolar 380 V DC platform to integrate 16 kW of solar, a 10 kW natural-gas generator, a 20 kW/100 kWh vanadium-redox flow battery, grid service, and DC/AC loads. The battery converter regulated the DC bus while distributed source and load controls coordinated without relying on a central real-time communications link.
burlington-dc-microgrid.status: Demonstration commissioned around 2017. ARDA still lists the Burlington C&I deployment on its project page, but no public source verifies current operation or performance through 2026.

equipment.detail: 20 kW/100 kWh vanadium-redox flow-battery system
Bidirectional DC/DC converter that exclusively regulates DC-bus voltage in the battery-centric architecture
Unidirectional MPPT DC/DC interfaces for the two photovoltaic arrays
A 230 W LED fixture replacing a 400 W metal-halide fixture, an 18,000 BTU air conditioner, and a 48 V mini-refrigerator were among documented controllable loads

technicalDetails.detail: At the heart of the Microgrid is a vanadium redoxflow battery supplied by GILDEMEISTER energy storage GmbH.
The battery is connected to the DC bus by built-in ARDA Battery DC-DC Converters.
​The Energy Storage system maintains the DC bus voltage and controls the Energy Management function of the Microgrid.
The distributed generation components linked to the DC bus will include 16KW of solar and 10KW of gas generation.
Both Sources will be integrated into the DC Microgrid using the ARDA Source DC-DC Converters, which in the case of Solar include maximum power point tracking (MPPT) .

The battery energy-storage converter forms and regulates the DC bus. Sources and loads respond locally to bus voltage, allowing real-time coordination without communications; supervisory communications are used for optimization, monitoring, and data collection.
ARDA describes the platform as capable of seamless transition between grid-connected and islanded operation, with the battery remaining the bus-voltage reference in both modes.
A PLC could shed or dim loads based on system conditions while bidirectional interfaces supported AC and DC loads on the common bus.

Gentherm acquired Etratech in November 2017 and said it would continue operations at the Burlington headquarters; this establishes a host ownership change, not the microgrid's current operating status.

camp-pendleton-microgrid.summary: The assigned Camp Pendleton record is the Area 52 School of Infantry FractalGrid demonstration, not later Camp Pendleton microgrids. Four distributed microgrids combined fixed and concentrated solar, diesel backup, flywheel and GEL-battery storage, vehicle-to-grid capability, and CleanSpark controls across a 12 kV system that could coordinate as a roughly 1.1 MW-class site microgrid.
camp-pendleton-microgrid.status: Area 52 was commissioned in 2014 and CleanSpark reported continuous operation for more than three years in 2017. CleanSpark expected release to the Marine Corps in 2018, but no public source verifies the final transfer or continued operation through 2026.
camp-pendleton-microgrid.historicalLocation: Camp Pendleton South, CA, United States

Distributed controls formed four separate microgrids at Area 52 and allowed them to align autonomously as a larger 12 kV microgrid, island from the utility, and reconnect after restoration.
CleanSpark reported in late 2017 that the mPulse system had operated continuously for more than three years with minimal maintenance.

CleanSpark bought the control infrastructure from Harper for $1 and assumed a ten-year operating/maintenance obligation. A 2018 filing said the company expected to release the assets to the Marine Corps that year, but that forward-looking statement is not proof the transfer closed.
The 1.1 MW figure in CleanSpark filings is the coordinated site microgrid scale and should not be added to the 525 kW solar and 200 kW diesel figures as another generator.
A separate Critical Infrastructure Support microgrid accepted in 2019 used about 150 kW solar and 400 kWh iron-flow storage. It is not the 2014 Area 52 FractalGrid represented by this record.

California announced a separate Haybarn Energy Reliability Center in December 2024: $42 million for an initial 6 MW/48 MWh battery system, expandable to 50 MW/400 MWh, with operation expected in summer 2027. This does not establish the current status of Area 52.

camp-smith-microgrid.summary: Camp H.M. Smith in Hawaii was the third and most installation-wide phase of the DoD/DOE/DHS SPIDERS demonstration. Completed in late 2015, it integrated the base's utility service, four new Tier 4 interim diesel generators, selected existing generators, a 300 kW photovoltaic array, strategically placed battery storage, secure supervisory controls, and fast-transfer power electronics to sustain critical Pacific Command loads.
camp-smith-microgrid.status: SPIDERS Phase III completed and successfully demonstrated in late 2015. Marine Corps Base Hawaii still lists the Camp Smith micro-grid as an energy innovation, but no public Camp Smith-specific operating or performance data through 2026 were found.
camp-smith-microgrid.historicalLocation: Camp H M Smith, Halawa, HI, United States

equipment.detail: Peer-to-peer controller nodes sit with microgrid equipment and automatically reconfigure when components change or fail.
High-speed transfer switches and controls transfer load to batteries in less than one electrical cycle before synchronizing generation.
Secure supervisory control overlay integrating existing SCADA/PLCs, generator dispatch, renewable resources, storage, load shedding, and resynchronization
Instantaneous synchronization/islanding module maintaining load through transfers under one electrical cycle and synchronizing generators after outage detection
Four Tier 4 interim stationary diesel generators, supplemented by a selected subset of the base's existing generator fleet

technicalDetails.detail: Phase III demonstrated installation-scale islanding, black start, renewable integration, generator dispatch, power-factor correction, peak management, load shedding, and secure reconnection with Hawaiian Electric.

GridMaster used an enclave architecture over existing control assets and underwent national-laboratory vulnerability and red-team evaluation. It received a DoD Platform IT Risk Approval/Authorization to Operate in August 2015, reported as the first DoD microgrid-control cybersecurity accreditation.

DOE's final report states that all three SPIDERS phases successfully operated their microgrids and met the joint demonstration goals; it does not publish a Camp Smith-specific post-2015 fuel-savings data series.

The report suggests that roughly 30% fossil-fuel reduction during long outages could be a reasonable program expectation, but this was not presented as measured Camp Smith performance and should not be treated as such.
Marine Corps Base Hawaii's current resources page continues to identify the Camp Smith micro-grid among installation energy innovations, but provides no operating statistics or refurbishment history.

cannon-afb.summary: The historical Cannon AFB microgrid record corresponds to a FY2011 Sandia energy-surety conceptual design/assessment funded by DOE's Office of Electricity and the Department of Defense. Sandia's later portfolio places it among military conceptual studies under 1 MW, not among operational prototypes. No public design package, equipment selection, construction award, or commissioned microgrid was found.
cannon-afb.status: Conceptual assessment only in FY2011; no evidence it advanced to construction. Later Cannon solar-efficiency and hydrogen-backup projects are separate resilience efforts, not proof that the FY2011 microgrid was built.

Compact solid-state hydrogen energy-production backup system deployed for the base water-supply system in June 2026

technicalDetails.detail: Sandia's 2017 portfolio explicitly lists Cannon AFB as a FY2011 DOE Office of Electricity/DoD conceptual design or assessment under 1 MW, while operational prototypes and large demonstrations are shown in separate categories.
A 2010 Sandia presentation listed Cannon among installations expressing FY2011 project interest; the later 2017 portfolio confirms an assessment occurred but provides no evidence of implementation.

The Air Force/DLA Energy awarded Ameresco an ESPC in June 2021 covering five conservation measures across 119 buildings and a 1.9 MW PV array. Announcements targeted Q1 2023 completion, but the reviewed official sources do not confirm final completion or describe the project as an islandable microgrid.

On June 4, 2026 the Air Force demonstrated Prometheus Hydrogen's HEGO backup system for Cannon's water supply. The solid-state hydrogen system is being evaluated as an alternative to mobile and stationary diesel backup generators; the Air Force calls it a pilot, not the FY2011 microgrid.

As of June 2026 Cannon still relied on mobile and stationary diesel generators for outage backup and was piloting hydrogen for one water-supply application. Public sources do not establish a base-wide islandable microgrid or storage fleet.
The FY2011 conceptual microgrid, the 2021 solar/controls ESPC, and the 2026 water-system hydrogen backup pilot are three distinct efforts and should not have their capacities or status merged.

catalina-island.summary: Southern California Edison's isolated Santa Catalina Island grid is centered on the Pebbly Beach Generating Station and combines diesel generation, propane microturbines, and a sodium-sulfur battery. The best public system inventory identifies 9.4 MW of diesel generation, 23 microturbines totaling about 1.5 MW, and a 1 MW/7.2 MWh battery. SCE converted all station generator fuel to renewable diesel in 2024 and is replacing three of six older diesel units to meet tighter air limits while continuing to evaluate a longer-term repower. Solar-plus-storage scenarios in the NREL repower study were alternatives, not installed equipment.
catalina-island.status: Operating as an isolated SCE utility system. SCE reports that all station generator fuel changed to renewable diesel in 2024 and that three of six older diesels are being replaced. A 2022 all-source procurement produced no compliant repower bid, so SCE was still evaluating propane and solar options in its current 2026 project account. The sodium-sulfur battery itself had not been declared end-of-life, but SCE told the CPUC in 2024 that its battery control system was inoperable because replacement parts were unavailable; a later return-to-service date was not located.
catalina-island.historicalLocation: Santa Catalina Island, Los Angeles County, CA, United States
